blurb: The classic bright-red pie cherry — the Front Range's bulletproof, time-tested tart cherry.
seasons: White blossom spring; bright-red tart cherries late June–July
wildlife: Bee-pollinated; birds eagerly take the fruit
toxic: Fruit flesh edible (tart); pits, leaves & twigs cyanogenic — toxic to people/pets/livestock
origin: Europe to SW Asia (Black Sea and Caspian region)
habitat: Forest margins and woodland edges in Europe and SW Asia

Spring — a single five-petaled flower face-on · © Cephas (CC BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Summer — bright-red Montmorency pie cherries · © Martha Dol. (CC BY 2.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Winter — the smooth grey-brown bark and branch structure · © PumpkinSky (CC BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons

care.hardiness: Hardy across USDA zones 3–8, which makes Montmorency a safe, time-tested choice for Boulder's 5b–6a winters and the standard tart cherry recommended for eastern Colorado.[3][1] On the Front Range, site it in a sheltered spot with gradual fall cool-down and a steady winter — abrupt temperature swings, not absolute cold, are what stress fruit trees here.[4]
care.planting: Plant in spring: set bare-root trees out early while still dormant, and container-grown trees after mid-May once hard-frost danger has passed.[2] Pick full sun on slightly higher ground so cold air drains away from the early bloom — a frost pocket can wipe out a crop — and avoid the warm south/west wall of a building, which pushes bloom dangerously early.[1] Dig the hole as deep as the rootball and twice as wide and water in well. In a pot Montmorency grows fine for a season, but a container freezes far harder than open ground over a Front Range winter and can kill roots even on a zone-3 tree, so use a large tub (≥20 gal), sink or heel it in, or overwinter it in an unheated shelter, and plan to plant it out the next spring.[2]
care.sun: Full sun — 6 or more hours of direct sun a day for the best crop and a healthy tree.[3][2] Montmorency colors and ripens well in Boulder's strong high-altitude light; shade reduces both flowering and fruit quality.[2]
care.soil: Wants fertile, well-drained soil and will not tolerate standing water — never plant where water pools after rain.[3][2] It handles the Front Range's alkaline clay-loam where drainage is adequate; loosen heavy clay and work in organic matter at planting to get it established. Good drainage matters more than soil richness.[3][2]
care.water: Water young trees about 1–2 inches a week (rain plus irrigation) through establishment.[2] Once settled it is fairly drought-tolerant, but deep soaks during dry summer stretches — especially while the fruit is sizing — keep the crop full. Avoid chronically wet roots.[2]
care.spacing: A standard tree reaches roughly 15–18 ft tall and wide; semi-dwarf forms stay near 10 ft.[2] Space standard trees about 15–20 ft apart (closer for semi-dwarf), giving each an open, sunny, frost-drained position.[2]
care.propagation: Montmorency does not come true from seed — a pit won't reproduce the cultivar.[3] It is propagated by grafting or budding onto a rootstock (commonly Mahaleb or Mazzard, or a dwarfing stock for the semi-dwarf form); home gardeners buy nursery trees rather than raising their own. It is a stable, centuries-old cultivar, so every Montmorency tree is a clone of the same plant.[3]
care.bloom: White five-petaled flowers open in spring, borne in clusters of three to five.[3] As a tart cherry it blooms a bit later than sweet cherries, which helps it slip past the Front Range's late frosts — though an early warm wall or a frost pocket can still nip the bloom.[1][4] Best of all it is self-fruitful: one tree sets a full crop with no second variety needed for pollination.[1]
care.feeding: Not a heavy feeder. A light spring application of balanced fertilizer is enough if growth is weak or foliage is pale; skip it on a vigorous tree.[2] Go easy on nitrogen — excess forces soft growth, cuts fruiting, and invites disease.[2]
care.maintenance: Train to an open-center (vase) form for light and airflow, and prune in late winter once the worst cold is past — dormant pruning in dry weather limits the fungal and bacterial cankers that spread when cuts are made wet.[2][1] Prune only when it's dry; if you must cut during the season, sterilize tools between cuts.[1] Remove dead, damaged, and crossing wood and keep the center open. Net the tree as fruit colors up — birds are the worst harvest pest.[2]
care.troubles: Birds will clean out a ripe crop, so plan to net it.[2] Cherry/black aphids and the cherry fruit fly are the main insects — destroy dropped fruit to break the fruit fly's cycle.[2] Brown rot and cherry leaf spot are the chief diseases; the dry Front Range climate, open-center pruning for airflow, and cleanup of fallen fruit and leaves keep them down.[2] Bacterial canker and cytospora canker strike stressed or wet-pruned trees — prune dry and dormant and cut cankered wood well below the lesion; a badly cankered trunk may have to be removed.[1][2]
care.harvest: Montmorency ripens late June into July on the Front Range — earlier than many cherries.[2][1] The fruit is bright glossy red with clear yellow flesh and a sharp, classic tart-cherry flavor: this is *the* American pie cherry, also used for jam, juice, and drying.[1] Pick when fully colored, before the birds finish it, clipping with stems on to protect the fruiting spurs.[2]
care.companions: Site it where bees can work the spring bloom — cherries are insect-pollinated.[1] Keep a mulched, grass-free zone over the root area rather than running turf to the trunk, and underplant only with low, non-competitive perennials and bulbs. Give it an open, sunny, frost-drained spot instead of crowding it under larger shade trees.[4][1]

edible.card: Tart pie cherries — pies, jam, juice
edible.summary: The ripe cherry flesh is edible — Montmorency is the classic pie/jam/juice cherry, usually cooked and sweetened — but the pit inside each cherry, plus the leaves and twigs, are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[1][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food. Montmorency cherries are bright red and mouth-puckeringly tart, the standard cherry for pies, preserves, jam, juice, and drying.[1] The stone (pit) inside each cherry, the leaves, and the twigs are not food and should never be eaten.[3]
edible.preparation: Pit the cherries before use — remove the stones and do not crush or chew them — then cook and sweeten the flesh, which is far too tart to enjoy raw.[1][3] Pitted, cooked, sweetened fruit (pie filling, jam, syrup, juice) is the standard and safe preparation.[1]
edible.caution: The pits (stones), leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic compounds that release hydrogen cyanide when chewed, crushed, or wilted — the pits are specifically poisonous.[3] Never swallow, crush, or chew the pits; always pit the fruit before cooking, and keep children and pets from gnawing seeds or stems. Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is the most dangerous form and is a documented poisoning hazard to livestock (cattle, horses) and to cats and dogs — don't let animals browse the tree or eat fallen prunings.[3]
